Methodology

How to use this tool

Overview: The Overview page presents all concepts as a cross-reference matrix. Each coloured pill links to the Lexicon. Cluster headings are clickable and navigate to the corresponding Lexicon tab. Toggle the By jurisdiction view to see all terms per legal system.

Lexicon: Use the tab bar to select a regulatory cluster. Open a concept card to view verbatim definitions side by side. The CEPS interpretative note appears in the blue panel on the left. Click ⇄ Comparative Analysis to see a structured comparison table. Use Verbatim ↗ to read the full quoted legal text for any section. Underlined terms are cross-references; click to navigate.

Law Sources: Provides links to official legislative texts. Sources marked Embeddable can be previewed inline via the Law Panel when clicking an article reference badge in the Lexicon.

Analytical framework

This lexicon maps foundational AI governance definitions across the EU AI Act and ten US state laws selected for their comprehensive, cross-sectoral AI governance scope. The EU AI Act serves as the primary benchmark, with US state laws assessed for conceptual convergence and divergence.

Definitions are reproduced verbatim where available, or paraphrased by CEPS analysts where the legislative text requires contextualisation. All paraphrased entries are labelled Summary; verbatim citations are labelled Verbatim.

For selected concept pairs, a structured Comparative Analysis table is provided, drawn from CEPS analytical work. These tables compare obligations, scope, and enforcement mechanisms across the EU AI Act and US state laws, and include full interpretative notes.

Data version

This version of the Digital Lexicon incorporates terminology and analysis from the second edition of the CEPS mapping exercise, covering the EU AI Act and five US state laws: California (SB53, SB942, AB2013), Colorado (SB24-205), New York (S8828), Texas (HB149), and Utah (SB226). The tool was updated in April 2026.

Selection criteria

US state laws were selected based on: comprehensive cross-sectoral AI governance scope; signed or approved status; and relevance of defined terminology to the EU AI Act's conceptual framework. Sector-specific laws were excluded.
